From <[https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-2-sector-innovations-and-transformers-ai-1698/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/resource/topic-2-sector-innovations-and-transformers-ai-1698/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp)>

## Rothman, D. (2024) Transformers for Natural Language Processing and Computer Vision, Packt, Third Edition (Chapters 1 & 2)

Attention is all you need gave rise to the transformer which is trained faster and gave better outputs

Recurrent Neural Networks(RNNs) and Convolutional Neural Networks(CNNs) would require lots of machine power.

Markov Decision Processes(MDPs), Markov Chains and Markov Processes. Markov showed that you can predict the next element of a chain using only the last past element. This was applied to letters.

Claude Shannon's The Mathematical theory of communication. Communicating model based on a source encoder, a transmitter and received decoder or semantic decoder.

Alan Turning Computing Machinery and Intelligence. Implementing AI to decode encrypted encoded messages in German.

IMM converted Russian sentence into English using a rule system.

John Hopfield introduced Recurrent Neural Networks(RNNs)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Each state Sn captures the information of Sn-1. When the end of the network is reached, a function F will perform an action: transduction, modelling or any other type of sequence-based task.

Yann Le Cun designed Convolutional Neural Network(CNN). Produced LeNet-5 which lead to many CNN models we know today.

### Attention is all you need

The original transformer model.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Recurrence has been abandoned from transformers. It's been replaced by attention.

Attention finds out how each word is related to all other words in a sequence including itself.

Attention will run a dot product between word vectors and determine the strongest relationships, including itself.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Attention provides a deeper relationship between words and produces better results.

To increase performance eight attention mechanisms are run in parallel. Multi-head attention

Broader in-depth analysis of sequences

Preclusion of recurrence reducing calculation operations

Implements parallelization, which reduces training time

Each attention mechanism learns different perspectives of the same input sequence.

So attention replaced recurrence.

### The encoder stack

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The encoder layer is the same for all of the N-6 layers of the transformer model. Each layer contains two main sub-layers: a multi-head attention mechanism and a fully connected position-wise feed forward network.

There are residual connections which bring the unprocessed inputs to the normalisation function. That way key information like positional encoding is not lost.

LayerNormalisation(x+sublayer(x))

The structure of N-6 layers is identical but the content is not strictly identical.

Each layer learns from the previous layer and explores different ways of associating the tokens in the sequence.

The output of every sub-layer has the same dimensions, d=512. This has many benefits Many key operations are dot products.  Reduces the number of operations to calculate, easier to trace the information as it flows through the model.

### Input embedding

Converts input token to vectors of dimension d=512

Works like other transduction models.

Tokenize

['the', 'transform', 'er', 'is', 'an', 'innovative', 'n', 'l', 'p', 'model', '!']

There will be an integer representation

Text = "The cat slept on the couch.It was too tired to get up."

tokenized text= [1996, 4937, 7771, 2006, 1996, 6411, 1012, 2009, 2001, 2205, 5458, 2000, 2131, 2039, 1012]

Use an embedding layer.

Skip-gram architecture of word2vec is an embedding approach.

For word(i) is the center word in a two step window, a skip-gram model will analyze word(i-2), word(i-2), word(i+1), word(i+2).

A skip-gram model generally contains an input layer, weights, hidden layer and output containing the word embeddings of the tokenized input words.

### Positional encoding

Add positional encoding value to the input embedding instead of having additional vectors.

Use a unit sphere to represent position encoding with sin and cosine values.

Position encoding(PE) for each position and each dimension I of d = 512

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Sin is used for even numbers and cosine used for odd numbers( of i )

There are other methods, could just do first half with sin and rest with cosine

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

If you just add them, then some of the word embedding information might be minimised.

Various ways to add them, one way is

Y1(black)*sqrt(d_model)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Sub-layer 1: Multi-head attention

Contains eight heads and is followed by a post-layer normalization, which add residual connections to the output of the sub-layer and normalize it.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Input is a vector with the embedding positional encoding. The next layers of the stack do not start these operations over.

Split each d=512 of each word into 8 dk=64 dimensions.

It uses Q, K and V to project it into 8 heads, so it's not just split, but linearly projected into a lower-dimensional space, unique to each head.

- Head 1 might learn to attend to verbs when processing queries.
- Head 2 might focus on named entities.
- Head 3 might track long-range dependencies.

- Head 1 = edge detection
- Head 2 = color enhancement
- Head 3 = brightness adjustment

Can run those 8 heads in parallel to speed up the training  and obtain 8 different representation subspaces of how each word relates to another.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The output of each head is a matrix zi with shape x*dk. The output of multi-attention head is Z

Z=(z0,z1…z7)

However, Z must be concatenated so that the output of the multi-head sublayer is not a sequence of dimensions but one lines of xm*dmodel matrix.

MultiHead(output) = Concat(z0, z1, z2, z3, z4, z5, z6, z7,) = x, dmodel

Inside each head, each word vector has three representations

Q, a query vector d=64, which is activated and trained when a word vector x seeks all of the key-value pairs of the other word vectors including itself in self-attention

K, a key vector d=64, which will be trained to provide an attention value

V, a value vector d=64, which will be trained to provide another attention value

Attention is a scaled dot-product attention.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

To obtain Q, K and V we need to train the model with their respective weight matrices. e.g. Q is obtained by a dotproduct between x, Qw

Represent the input

Code

[PacktPublishing/Transformers-for-Natural-Language-Processing: Transformers for Natural Language Processing, published by Packt](https://github.com/PacktPublishing/Transformers-for-Natural-Language-Processing)

- Query (Q): Represents what we’re looking for — it’s the "question" the model is asking.
- Key (K): Represents what each word offers — like a label or identifier.
- Value (V): Contains the actual content or information of the word.

- The query is your search request (what you're looking for).
- The key is the label on each book.
- The value is the content of each book.

You transform the embedding into value,  can emphasize more aspects, e.g. syntactic tasks, emphasize grammatical role, for semantic tasks, highlight meaning.

For multi head each Wv is different, so they are extracting different things about the value

Concat, is a horizontal concation. So number of rows are the same but numbers of columns are different.

Concat with

np.hstack

### Post-layer normalization

Feedforward sub-layer. Each sublayer is followed by post-layer normalization(Post-LN)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x))

There are many layer normalization methods.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Sub-layer 2: Feedforward network

![](data:image/png;base64,)

FFN in the encoder and decoder are fully connected

FFN is a position-wise network. Each position is processed separately and in an identical way.

FFN contains two layers and applies a ReLU activation function

The input and output of the FFN layers is d=512 but the inner layer is larger with dff=2048

FFN can be viewed as performing two kernel size 1 convolutions

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### The decoder stack

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Same as the encoder for N=6 layers. Each layer contains three sub-layers, multi0headed masked attention mechanism, a multi-headed attention mechanism, and a fully connected position-wise feedforward network

You mask during training.

InMa the sequence.

Cross attention

|   |   |   |
|---|---|---|
|Component|Comes from|Why?|
|Q|Decoder|It’s asking: "What do I need next?"|
|K|Encoder output|Represents "what's available to match against"|
|V|Encoder output|Gives the content (values) once the attention weights are computed|

### The attention layers

The transformer is an auto-regressive model. It uses previous output sequences as an additional input.

The first layer doesn't have future words, so it forces it to learn to predict.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### FFN sub-layer, Post-LN and linear layer

FFN same as in encoder stack, so is Post-LN

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Linear layer

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Where x and b are learned parameters

Linear layer produces the next probable elements of a sequence that softwax will convert into a probable element.

So linear is like a score, softmax makes it a probability. Might be at the level of layers.

### Training and performance

Training the original transformer model took 12 hours to train 100,000 steps on a machine with 9 P100 GPUs. The big models took 3.5 days for 300,000 steps.

# Main

## AI in business

Energy. Renewables. Move from centralised plants to microgrids. This requires intelligent devises that can monitor and manage demand in real time.

Health. AI can help with preventative medicine rather than reactive.

AI can provide greater access.

AI can help with personalisation.

Education. More modern learning, more immersive, more digital and collaborative learning.

Move from traditional education to practical skill-based education, with focus on ethics, life-long learning, innovation and creativity.

Agriculture. Increase productivity while reducing environmental impact. Autonomous robots, drones, machine vision, etc. Selecting ripe tomatoes. Plant based mean and 3d printing.

Manufacturing. Industrial Internet of Things(IIIT), predictive maintenance, Automation, robots and cobots, 3d printing, etc.

Supply chain. Electric and autonomous vehicles

Finance. Finance apps, non-traditional provider, etc.

AI - natural language queries, categorising data. Detecting LLM.

## Transformer models

Recurrent Neural Networks(RNNs) analyse tokens in sequences.

Transformers where each token is related to all the other other tokens in a sequence.

One that has been trained on billions of records and has billions of parameters is known as a foundation model. Like GPT, claude, llama, etc.

### Industry 4.0

Industry 4.0 refers to automation and digitisation of manufacturing and industrial process through IoT, AI, big data, robotics and cloud computing.

Smart factories, where machines can make decisions autonomously.

First: Mechanisation - steam and water

Second: Mass production - electricity and assembly line

Third: Computer and automation - electrons, computer and robotics

Fourth: Cyber-physical system, data exchange and AI with new ways for humans and machine to interact.

AI specialists will need to add prompt engineering.

### Prompt engineering

Sometimes models have their own rules or metalanguage so it's not always just free-flowing natural language.

Industry 4.9 AI has blurred the lines between cloud platforms, frameworks, libraries, languages and models.

Google TRAX or Open AI Transformer API.

### Tokenisation

Breaks down a sentence into words or other subparts.

NLTK has tokenise method, which breaks it down to sentences or words.

### Word embedding

Word embedding is representing a word as a vector.

Embedding is a dense vector of gloating point values.

Embeddings are trainable, including unsupervised learning.

A neural network would be trained on say 10 words with the middle missing, and the model needs to predict it. Each word has it's own vector.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

When trained weight becomes values in a vector.

Word2Vec by Mikolov allowed it to be done with less computational complexity.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

GloVe was another method, it is more explicit, encoding meaning as vector offsets in an embedding space

It looks at the ratio of co-occurrence probabilities of two words(rather than the co-occurrence probabilities themselves) is what contains information, so look to encode this information as vector differences. It trains on word co-occurrence statistics for a corpus rather than the corpus itself.

In NLP you can have machine learn an embedding through training or use ready made embedding.

Learning an embedding

1. Standalone. Good if using the same embedding in multiple models.
2. Learn jointly. As part of specific project.

Reuse an embedding

1. Static. Use embedding as is.
2. Updated. Embedding is updated jointly during the training of the model.

### Transformer architecture

Encoder-decoder architecture.

In sequence-to-sequence operations inputs and outputs can be variable length. This can be handled by encoder-decoder architecture

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The original transformer model is a stack of six layers.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The attention mechanism

It finds out how each word is related to all the other words in a sequence.

It will run a dot product between word vectors and determine the strongest relationships of a word with all other words.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Each attention sublayer runs eight attention mechanisms in parallel to speed up the calculation.

In-deph analysis of the sequences

Implementation of parallelism.

Each attention mechanisms learns different perspectives of the same input sequence.

Each layer has this structure

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Each layer learns from the previous layer and explores different ways of associating the tokens.

Input embedding.

Input tokens to vectors of dimension 512.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Activity: Word embedding

Read page 23-24 of the set text to understand the process (Chapter 2: The Rise of the Transformer – Attention is all You Need – the encoder stack sub-section on input embedding).

Rothman, D. (2024). Transformers for Natural Language Processing. Packt Publishing Ltd. Available via Kortext.

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/transformer-architecture-ai/transformer-architecture/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/transformer-architecture-ai/transformer-architecture/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp)>

Can use cosine similarity to see if words are similar.

### Position embedding

Information about the order of words.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Read the section on positional encoding from the set text, pages 24-29. Chapter 2: The Rise of the Transformer – Attention is all You Need)

Rothman, D. (2021). Transformers for Natural Language Processing. Packt. Available via Kortext.

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/transformer-architecture-ai/position-embedding/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/transformer-architecture-ai/position-embedding/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp)>

![](data:image/png;base64,)

If the positional ecoding drowns out the word embedding you can cope with that with

pc(black) = y1 * math.sqrt(dmodel)

From <[https://app.qa.com/course/transformer-architecture-ai/position-embedding/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp](https://app.qa.com/course/transformer-architecture-ai/position-embedding/?context_id=15580&context_resource=lp)>

### Multi-head attention

Eight heads and normalisation layer which will add residual connection to output of the sublayer and normalise it.

The dmodel 512 dimensions of each word is divided into 8 d=64 dimensions

In each head the attention mechanism the word matrices have three representations

Eight heads obtain eight different representation subspaces of how each word relates to another.

1. A query matrix q has dimension 64, which seeks all the key-value pairs of the other word matrices
2. A key matrix K, has dimension of 64 which will be trained to provide an attention value
3. A value matrix v has dimension 64, which will be trained to provide another attention value

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Q, K, V are obtained by training the model with weight matrics Qw, Kw, Vw

e.g. Q is dot product between x(input word vector) and Qw

Post-layer normalisation

Each attention sublayer and each feedforward sublayer is followed by post-layer normalisation(Post-LN)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### Feedforward network

The input of feedforward network(FFN) is dmodel = 512 of post-LN of the previous sublayer

![](data:image/png;base64,)

The FFN in the encoder and decoder are fully connected. The FFN is a position-wide network where each position is processed separately and in an identical way, where each FFN contains two layers and applies the Rectified Linear Unit(RELU) activation function

The input and output of FFN is d=512 but the inner layer is larger with d=2048

The output of the FFN goes to Post-LN

The output of the Post-LN then goes to the next layer of the encoder stack and the multi-head attention layer of the decoder stack.

### The decoder stack

Layered like the encoder layers. The structure of the decoder layer remains the same as the encoder for all N=6 layers of the transformer model.

The decoder has a third main sublayer which is the masked multi-head mechanism. In this sublayer, subsequent words are masked so that the transformer bases it's assumptions on its inferences without seeing the rest of the sequence and hence can learn to predict. The output words go through the word embedding layer and then the positional encoding function like in the first layer of the endoding stack.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Input attention = (Ouput_decoder_sublayer - 1(Q), Output_encoder_layer(K,V))

Q, K, V - Query, Key and Value

-1 refers to the missing word.

The query goes into the Multi-head attention. There are two attention matrices from the encoder, one for keys and one for values.

Both will have been created from the training data input to the encoder where they will have been through layers of multi-head attention and feedforward network.

During the self-attention calculation, the queries are compared to the keys to produce attention scores, which are then used to weight the values. The weighted values are then summed together to produce the final output of the self-attention layer.

The transform then goes to the feedforward network sublayer, followed by a post-layer normalisation, or post-LN and linear layer.

The transformer produces an output sequence of only one element at a time

The linear layer produces and output sequence with a a linear function that varies per model but relies on the standard method(w and b are learn parameters)

The linear layer will thus produce the next probable elements of a sequence that a softmax function will convert into a probabilities.

Output sequence = (y1, y2.. Yn)

y= w*x + b

### Transformer summary

Transformers work by encoding word sequences into a vector space

The decoder is fully linked to the encoder and can be trained to predict the next word in a sequence

The encoder and decoder have similar architectures

The encoding is based on the relationship a word has with its neighbours in a sequence and the position of the word in a sequence.

Semantics get captured by based on mathematical operations(many dot products). The attention mechanism is key to capturing the relationship between words and is an important innovation in this space.

The attention method supports parallel processing, which contributes to usability and efficiency.
